title: Appendicitis
oneLiner: Acute inflammation of the appendix causing right iliac fossa pain; common surgical emergency in young adults; early recognition prevents perforation and sepsis.

sections.1. Pattern Recognition — The Patient You:
You’re on call. A 19-year-old, previously well, presents with 12 hours of dull central abdominal pain that over the last 6 hours has shifted to the right lower quadrant. She’s lost her appetite, vomited once, and now feels feverish. On exam, she has tenderness at McBurney’s point (one third of the way from ASIS to umbilicus), worse when you push and let go (rebound). Rovsing’s sign is present: left-sided pressure elicits pain on the right. Her temperature is 37.8°C, WBC 13.2 × 10⁹/L, CRP 45 mg/L. The urine dip is negative. The surgical registrar is about to put ultrasound.

**Dissecting the vignette – pattern clues and what they tell you:**

- **“Dull central pain shifting to RLQ”** — Early visceral pain from distended appendix (T10 dermatome) converts to somatic parietal pain when inflamed appendix touches the peritoneum of the right iliac fossa. This migration is the single most specific historical clue (>80% positive predictive value).
- **“Anorexia and vomiting”** — Reflex gastroparesis caused by peritoneal irritation; vomiting is typically not profuse but a reliable early feature. If vomiting is bilious or frequent, consider small-bowel obstruction or gastroenteritis instead.
- **“Low-grade fever + tachycardia if peritonitis”** — Inflammatory response to bacterial translocation; high fever >39°C suggests perforation with abscess or generalised peritonitis.
- **“Localised tenderness + rebound”** — Sign that parietal peritoneum is involved. Guarding indicates involuntary muscle spasm – a harder sign that suggests perforation.
- **“Rovsing’s – left pressure causes right pain”** — Gas or contents are pushed across to the inflamed appendix, confirming peritoneal irritation.
- **“Elevated WBC and CRP”** — Typical acute-phase response; a completely normal CRP + WBC makes appendicitis unlikely (<5% probability in most series).

**Atypical presentations that may trick you:**

1. **Elderly patient (≥60 years):** Pain less localised, often blunted; presents late with vague discomfort, confusion, low-grade fever. The appendix may already be perforated on arrival. Do not rely on classical signs – any unexplained sepsis with RLQ or even central pain should prompt CT.
2. **Pregnant patient (especially 2nd/3rd trimester):** The appendix shifts up and laterally (toward the right flank or even RUQ) as the uterus expands. Pain may mimic cholecystitis or pyelonephritis. Ultrasound may be limited; MRI is preferred if needed.
3. **Child (under 5 years):** Presents with irritability, crying, pulling up legs. Examination is difficult – often no localisation early. High perforation rate (up to 80%) because diagnosis is delayed. Consider ultrasound and low-threshold for CT if clinical suspicion remains.
sections.2. What Is This? (Definition & Why It Ma: Appendicitis is acute inflammation of the vermiform appendix, a blind-ending tube attached to the caecum. It is the most common acute surgical condition worldwide: lifetime risk is 7–8% and it accounts for over 50,000 emergency appendectomies per year in the UK alone. The critical stakes are that missed or delayed diagnosis leads to perforation (risk increases after 24–36 hours), causing peritonitis, abscess formation, and sepsis. Conversely, unnecessary appendectomy carries operative morbidity. The clinical challenge: no single test is perfect – you must combine history, exam, labs, and imaging.
sections.3. Pathophysiology — The \"Why\" Behind:
The story begins with **luminal obstruction** (most often by a faecolith – 40% of cases, or lymphoid hyperplasia – 20%, especially in children/viral illness). Obstruction traps mucus secretion —> pressure rises —> venous congestion —> mucosal ischaemia —> bacterial translocation (E. coli, Bacteroides) —> transmural inflammation —> sterile exudate that becomes purulent.

- **Why does pain move from central to RLQ?** Early distension stimulates visceral afferents (T10) – vague, dull, central. Once inflammation reaches the serosa, it irritates the parietal peritoneum (somatic fibres, L1–L2, localised to McBurney’s point).  
- **Why anorexia and vomiting?** Peritoneal irritation activates vagal and sympathetic reflexes, causing gastroparesis.  
- **Why fever and tachycardia?** Cytokine release (IL-6, TNF) and bacterial products drive the acute-phase response.  
- **Why Rovsing’s sign?** Left colon compression pushes gas distally, distending the caecum and tugging on the inflamed appendix, reproducing pain on the right.  
- **Why psoas and obturator signs?** The appendix lies retrocaecal (psoas sign – pain on hip extension) or pelvic (obturator sign – pain on internal rotation of flexed hip). Positive psoas sign raises suspicion of retrocaecal appendicitis, which can be masked and more likely to perforate.

This pathophysiologic progression is timed: pain shift suggests 6–12 hours of inflammation; guarding and rigidity indicate peritoneal irritation and imminent perforation (12–24 hours); high fever and diffuse tenderness suggest perforation with free air or abscess (24–36 hours).
sections.4. Causes / Triggers (organised in bins):
| Category | Cause | Stem clue to suggest this cause |
|---------|-------|----------------------------------|
| **Obstructive** (most common, ~70%) | Faecolith (calcified stool) | Young adult with constipation, palpable faecal mass on exam or on CT |
| | Lymphoid hyperplasia | Child or adolescent with preceding viral URTI; prominent Peyer’s patches |
| | Foreign body (e.g., seed, pin) | Odd clinical history; known ingestion |
| | Tumour (carcinoid, adenocarcinoma) | Older adult >50; chronic symptoms; atypical imaging mass |
| **Infectious** (rare primary cause) | Yersinia, Salmonella, Shigella | Associated diarrhoea, fever; may mimic gastroenteritis |
| | Measles (atypical) | Prodromal rash, cough, coryza |
| **Idiopathic** (~10–15%) | No identifiable obstruction | Diagnosis of exclusion; classic histology shows neutrophilic infiltration without luminal cause |

**Most common cause:** Faecolith. Look for it on CT: an appendicolith is a calcified density within the appendix lumen; it is present in ~40% of acute appendicitis cases and doubles the risk of perforation.
sections.5. Investigations — What, Why, What It M:
**Diagnostic scoring system: Alvarado Score (MANTRELS)**  
- M – Migration of pain (1 point)  
- A – Anorexia (1)  
- N – Nausea/vomiting (1)  
- T – Tenderness in RLQ (2)  
- R – Rebound pain (1)  
- E – Elevated temperature >37.3°C (1)  
- L – Leucocytosis >10 × 10⁹/L (2)  
- S – Shift to left (neutrophilia >75%) (1)  
*Score interpretation: ≤4 – low probability; 5–6 – equivocal, need imaging; 7–10 – high probability, consider surgery.*

**Pattern-recognition keys for each test:**

| Test | What It Shows | Why Order It | Interpretation |
|------|--------------|-------------|----------------|
| **WBC + differential** | Neutrophilic leucocytosis (often >11 × 10⁹/L, >75% neutrophils) | No single test rules in/out; part of Alvarado | Abnormal WBC supports inflammation; normal WBC does NOT exclude appendicitis (especially children, elderly). |
| **CRP** | Elevated >10 mg/L (usually >50 mg/L) | Highly sensitive (95%) but not specific | Rising CRP from normal to high over 12–24h is strongly suggestive; very high >150 mg/L suggests perforation or abscess. |
| **Ultrasound** | Target sign (oedematous wall), non-compressible appendix >6 mm, free fluid, appendicolith | First-line in children, pregnant women; no radiation | Operator-dependent; non-visualisation of appendix does NOT rule out (15–30% failure rate). Diagnostic if seen and non-compressible. |
| **CT abdomen (with IV contrast)** | Thickened appendiceal wall (>6 mm), fat stranding, appendix enhancing, appendicolith | Gold standard; sensitivity >95%, specificity >95% | The single finding that confirms appendicitis is a dilated appendix (>6 mm) with periappendiceal fat stranding. Absence of both rules it out with high certainty. Use in adults and equivocal cases. |
| **Urine dipstick** | Normal (occasional trace protein, ketones) | Rule out UTI/renal colic; pregnancy test essential | Blood or leucocytes suggest UTI or stone; hCG+ changes management (laparoscopy vs open? MRI vs US). |

**Pattern:** In a classic case (Alvarado ≥7), proceed to theatre; in equivocal (Alvarado 4–6), get CT. In a well-appearing child/young adult with typical pain shift, some surgeons go directly to laparoscopy without imaging (but CT remains gold standard for uncertainty).
sections.6. Management — Approach & Reasoning:
**Immediate (first hour):**  
- **NBM** (nil by mouth) – in case of surgery, to reduce aspiration risk under anaesthesia.  
- **IV fluids** – Hartmann’s or normal saline at maintenance (e.g., 125 mL/h) – to correct dehydration from vomiting and fever.  
- **Analgesia** – **morphine 2.5–5 mg IV if needed** – safe, does not mask signs (contrary to old dogma; multiple RCTs show no effect on diagnostic accuracy).  
- **Antibiotics (if suspected complicated appendicitis or pending surgery >12h)** – **co-amoxiclav 1.2 g IV TDS** (or, if penicillin allergy, metronidazole 500 mg IV TDS + gentamicin 5–7 mg/kg IV daily). Antibiotics reduce risk of wound infection and intra-abdominal abscess.  
- **Urgent surgical referral** – discuss a plan (immediate theatre? CT first?) with registrar.  
- **Do NOT** give antiemetics before surgical review – they can mask vomiting of obstruction.  
- **Do NOT** start broad-spectrum antibiotics without confirming diagnosis unless the patient is septic – may delay definitive treatment and mask progression.

**Ward (24–72h) – if surgery chosen:**  
- **Laparoscopic appendectomy** (preferred, less pain, faster recovery). If perforated – consider open if laparoscopic skill inadequate.  
- **Postoperative:** Continue IV fluids until tolerating oral; antibiotics for 24h (or longer if perforated – 3–5 days). Analgesia: regular paracetamol 1 g QDS IV/PO + ibuprofen 400 mg TDS PO if not contraindicated.  
- **If conservative (non-surgical) approach chosen** (uncomplicated appendicitis, no appendicolith): **piperacillin-tazobactam 4.5 g IV TDS for 48h** then transition to oral co-amoxiclav 625 mg TDS for 5–7 days.  
  - *Reasoning:* Antibiotics alone may resolve inflammation; 70% success at 1 year. But recurrence rate is 20–30% – most surgeons still recommend appendectomy.  
- **Monitor for failure:** worsening pain, fever, tachycardia – if no improvement in 24h, switch to surgical plan.

**Long-term:**  
- For the 7–8% who recur after conservative treatment: elective laparoscopic appendectomy.  
- For those with appendiceal tumour found incidentally (carcinoid <2 cm, no metastasis): appendectomy alone is curative. Larger or mesenteric invasion needs right hemicolectomy.  
- Educate the patient: after appendectomy can resume normal activity in 2–4 weeks; heavy lifting after 6 weeks if open.

**Common pitfalls:**  
- **Delaying surgery beyond 36 hours** – perforation risk increases from 20% to 70% after 48h.  
- **Giving antibiotics before CT when patient is stable** – may mask active inflammation, making diagnosis impossible.  
- **Ignoring the elderly** – early CT is the standard, not clinical scoring.  
- **Forgetting pregnancy test** in any woman of reproductive age – ectopic pregnancy can mimic appendicitis exactly, and you cannot operate without knowing.
sections.7. Differential Diagnosis — Comparison T:
Comparison of appendicitis to its three most important mimics.

| Feature | Appendicitis | Gastroenteritis | Mesenteric Adenitis | Ruptured Ectopic Pregnancy |
|---------|--------------|----------------|---------------------|----------------------------|
| **Pain** | Shifting from central to RLQ; gradual onset | Crampy, diffuse, often periumbilical with diarrhoea | Lower abdominal, may be more diffuse, worse on left? | Sudden one-sided pelvic pain with shoulder tip pain |
| **Exam** | Rebound at McBurney’s; Rovsing’s; may have psoas/obturator | Diffuse tenderness without guarding; hyperactive bowel sounds | Tenderness often in RLQ but without classic peritoneal signs | Cervical motion tenderness; adnexal mass on pelvic exam; Cullen’s sign late |
| **Temperature** | Low-grade (37.5–38.5°C) | Usually low; can be high if infectious | Low-grade, but can be higher in children | Normal to low (if hypovolaemic shock) |
| **Labs** | Moderate WBC/CRP; normal LFTs | WBC variable; low CRP early; stool culture + | WBC may be mildly elevated; CRP modest | Positive β-hCG; falling haematocrit; low WBC |
| **Imaging** | US: non-compressible appendix >6 mm; CT: fat stranding | Normal appendix on CT; colonic wall thickening | CT: multiple enlarged mesenteric nodes (>5 mm), normal appendix | Ultrasound: free fluid in pouch of Douglas, adnexal mass with/without cardiac activity; β-hCG >1500 IU/L with no intrauterine pregnancy |
| **Key distinguishing feature** | Pain migration + anorexia + RLQ tenderness | Diarrhoea (watery or bloody) before pain; no anorexia | Preceding viral illness in child; normal appendix on CT | Hypotension, positive pregnancy test, adnexal mass |
sections.8. Complications — What If You Miss It?:
- **Perforation** – presents with sudden worsening of pain, then generalised peritonitis (diffuse guarding, rigidity, silent abdomen). Fever spikes, tachycardia. Lactate rises. *What to do:* Call senior immediately; prepare for emergency laparotomy; start IV broad-spectrum antibiotics (e.g., piperacillin-tazobactam + metronidazole); resuscitation with fluids and vasopressors as needed.
- **Appendiceal abscess** – presents 3–5 days after symptom onset with swinging pyrexia, rigors, a palpable mass in the RLQ. *Key sign:* a tender, fixed mass on deep palpation – “appendiceal phlegmon” if early, or abscess if fluctuant. *What to do:* CT with IV contrast to confirm; percutaneous drainage if >3 cm; antibiotics; delayed appendectomy (6–8 weeks). Do NOT operate acutely – risk of spreading infected material.
- **Pylephlebitis** (septic thrombophlebitis of portal vein) – rare but life-threatening. Presents with high fever, rigors, jaundice, RUQ pain, and deranged LFTs. *Key sign:* CT shows thrombus in portal vein with surrounding inflammation. *What to do:* IV antibiotics (anaerobic coverage), anticoagulation, source control (appendectomy). May require ICU.
- **Late-presentation patterns:** elderly patient presents with confusion, ileus, and low-grade fever after a week of mild pain – the appendix has already perforated and a contained abscess may have ruptured, causing generalised peritonitis. Look for a “doughy” abdomen, marked distension, and hypotension. This stem tests recognition of “missed appendicitis” – do not be fooled by absence of classic pain.
sections.9. High-Yield Mnemonics & Pearls:
**Mnemonic for Alvarado Score (MANTRELS):**  
M – Migration (1)  
A – Anorexia (1)  
N – Nausea/vomiting (1)  
T – Tenderness RLQ (2)  
R – Rebound (1)  
E – Elevated temperature (1)  
L – Leukocytosis (2)  
S – Shift to left (1)

**Mnemonic for signs of peritonitis:** *“Grr, R, P, O”* – Guarding, Rebound, Psoas, Obturator. Plus “Silent abdomen” (no bowel sounds in generalised peritonitis).

**Clinical Pearls (pattern-recognition cues):**
1. **Pain migration** is the most reliable single history element – if it didn’t move, think again (e.g., mesenteric adenitis, renal colic, ovulation pain).
2. **Anorexia** is almost universal – a patient who is hungry does NOT have acute appendicitis until proven otherwise.
3. **Rovsing’s sign** is specific but not sensitive; if negative, it doesn’t rule out.
4. **Psoas sign** is a red flag for retrocaecal appendix – more likely to perforate early because it’s not walled off.
5. **Elderly + RLQ tenderness + fever = appendicitis until proven otherwise** regardless of pain pattern. CT first.
6. **Pregnant + RLQ pain = always think appendicitis** because it’s the most common non-obstetric surgical emergency in pregnancy. Delay is catastrophic for both mother and foetus.
7. **CT is the gold standard** – a negative CT with good quality IV contrast virtually rules out appendicitis. Do not operate on negative ultrasound alone.

**Common exam / ward mistakes:**
- Sending patient home with “gastroenteritis” because of mild diarrhoea – remember: appendicitis can have loose stools due to irritation of adjacent bowel.
- Using a urine dip to “rule out” – negative dip does not rule in appendicitis; positive dip can be from inflamed appendix adjacent to ureter (sterile pyuria).
- In elderly, treating for UTI based on positive dipstick without imaging – the pyuria may be sterile from adjacent inflammation; they need a CT.
sections.10. Escalation Criteria:
**When to call your senior / ICU / crash team:**

- **NEWS2 ≥5** (or NEWS ≥7) – any sign of clinical deterioration (tachycardia >120, hypotension <90 systolic, respiratory rate >25, oxygen saturations <92% on air, confusion).  
- **Lactate >2 mmol/L** (rising or >4 mmol/L suggests shock or mesenteric ischaemia, especially if perforated).  
- **GCS <15** or any drop from baseline (think sepsis, metabolic derangement, or intracranial bleed – especially if elderly).  
- **Signs of peritonitis** – generalised guarding, rigidity, silent abdomen – call senior to review for emergency laparotomy.  
- **Pregnant patient with suspected appendicitis and any hypotension or fetal distress** – call obstetric anaesthetist and senior surgeon.  
- **Worsening pain or fever despite antibiotics** (conservative management failure) – escalate to consultant surgeon for re-assessment and possible appendectomy.

**Specific thresholds for ICU:**  
- Septic shock requiring vasopressors (e.g., noradrenaline).  
- Acute kidney injury (oliguria, creatinine doubling).  
- Post-operative respiratory failure or need for prolonged ventilation.  

In your personal practice: if you are worried, escalate early. The appendix perforates on your watch if you hesitate.

## Appendicitis
**One-liner:** Acute inflammation of the appendix causing right iliac fossa pain; common surgical emergency in young adults; early recognition prevents perforation and sepsis.

### 1. Pattern Recognition — The Patient You'll See
You’re on call. A 19-year-old, previously well, presents with 12 hours of dull central abdominal pain that over the last 6 hours has shifted to the right lower quadrant. She’s lost her appetite, vomited once, and now feels feverish. On exam, she has tenderness at McBurney’s point (one third of the way from ASIS to umbilicus), worse when you push and let go (rebound). Rovsing’s sign is present: left-sided pressure elicits pain on the right. Her temperature is 37.8°C, WBC 13.2 × 10⁹/L, CRP 45 mg/L. The urine dip is negative. The surgical registrar is about to put ultrasound.

**Dissecting the vignette – pattern clues and what they tell you:**

- **“Dull central pain shifting to RLQ”** — Early visceral pain from distended appendix (T10 dermatome) converts to somatic parietal pain when inflamed appendix touches the peritoneum of the right iliac fossa. This migration is the single most specific historical clue (>80% positive predictive value).
- **“Anorexia and vomiting”** — Reflex gastroparesis caused by peritoneal irritation; vomiting is typically not profuse but a reliable early feature. If vomiting is bilious or frequent, consider small-bowel obstruction or gastroenteritis instead.
- **“Low-grade fever + tachycardia if peritonitis”** — Inflammatory response to bacterial translocation; high fever >39°C suggests perforation with abscess or generalised peritonitis.
- **“Localised tenderness + rebound”** — Sign that parietal peritoneum is involved. Guarding indicates involuntary muscle spasm – a harder sign that suggests perforation.
- **“Rovsing’s – left pressure causes right pain”** — Gas or contents are pushed across to the inflamed appendix, confirming peritoneal irritation.
- **“Elevated WBC and CRP”** — Typical acute-phase response; a completely normal CRP + WBC makes appendicitis unlikely (<5% probability in most series).

**Atypical presentations that may trick you:**

1. **Elderly patient (≥60 years):** Pain less localised, often blunted; presents late with vague discomfort, confusion, low-grade fever. The appendix may already be perforated on arrival. Do not rely on classical signs – any unexplained sepsis with RLQ or even central pain should prompt CT.
2. **Pregnant patient (especially 2nd/3rd trimester):** The appendix shifts up and laterally (toward the right flank or even RUQ) as the uterus expands. Pain may mimic cholecystitis or pyelonephritis. Ultrasound may be limited; MRI is preferred if needed.
3. **Child (under 5 years):** Presents with irritability, crying, pulling up legs. Examination is difficult – often no localisation early. High perforation rate (up to 80%) because diagnosis is delayed. Consider ultrasound and low-threshold for CT if clinical suspicion remains.

### 2. What Is This? (Definition & Why It Matters)
Appendicitis is acute inflammation of the vermiform appendix, a blind-ending tube attached to the caecum. It is the most common acute surgical condition worldwide: lifetime risk is 7–8% and it accounts for over 50,000 emergency appendectomies per year in the UK alone. The critical stakes are that missed or delayed diagnosis leads to perforation (risk increases after 24–36 hours), causing peritonitis, abscess formation, and sepsis. Conversely, unnecessary appendectomy carries operative morbidity. The clinical challenge: no single test is perfect – you must combine history, exam, labs, and imaging.

### 3. Pathophysiology — The "Why" Behind The Findings
The story begins with **luminal obstruction** (most often by a faecolith – 40% of cases, or lymphoid hyperplasia – 20%, especially in children/viral illness). Obstruction traps mucus secretion —> pressure rises —> venous congestion —> mucosal ischaemia —> bacterial translocation (E. coli, Bacteroides) —> transmural inflammation —> sterile exudate that becomes purulent.

- **Why does pain move from central to RLQ?** Early distension stimulates visceral afferents (T10) – vague, dull, central. Once inflammation reaches the serosa, it irritates the parietal peritoneum (somatic fibres, L1–L2, localised to McBurney’s point).  
- **Why anorexia and vomiting?** Peritoneal irritation activates vagal and sympathetic reflexes, causing gastroparesis.  
- **Why fever and tachycardia?** Cytokine release (IL-6, TNF) and bacterial products drive the acute-phase response.  
- **Why Rovsing’s sign?** Left colon compression pushes gas distally, distending the caecum and tugging on the inflamed appendix, reproducing pain on the right.  
- **Why psoas and obturator signs?** The appendix lies retrocaecal (psoas sign – pain on hip extension) or pelvic (obturator sign – pain on internal rotation of flexed hip). Positive psoas sign raises suspicion of retrocaecal appendicitis, which can be masked and more likely to perforate.

This pathophysiologic progression is timed: pain shift suggests 6–12 hours of inflammation; guarding and rigidity indicate peritoneal irritation and imminent perforation (12–24 hours); high fever and diffuse tenderness suggest perforation with free air or abscess (24–36 hours).

### 4. Causes / Triggers (organised in bins)

| Category | Cause | Stem clue to suggest this cause |
|---------|-------|----------------------------------|
| **Obstructive** (most common, ~70%) | Faecolith (calcified stool) | Young adult with constipation, palpable faecal mass on exam or on CT |
| | Lymphoid hyperplasia | Child or adolescent with preceding viral URTI; prominent Peyer’s patches |
| | Foreign body (e.g., seed, pin) | Odd clinical history; known ingestion |
| | Tumour (carcinoid, adenocarcinoma) | Older adult >50; chronic symptoms; atypical imaging mass |
| **Infectious** (rare primary cause) | Yersinia, Salmonella, Shigella | Associated diarrhoea, fever; may mimic gastroenteritis |
| | Measles (atypical) | Prodromal rash, cough, coryza |
| **Idiopathic** (~10–15%) | No identifiable obstruction | Diagnosis of exclusion; classic histology shows neutrophilic infiltration without luminal cause |

**Most common cause:** Faecolith. Look for it on CT: an appendicolith is a calcified density within the appendix lumen; it is present in ~40% of acute appendicitis cases and doubles the risk of perforation.

### 5. Investigations — What, Why, What It Means
**Diagnostic scoring system: Alvarado Score (MANTRELS)**  
- M – Migration of pain (1 point)  
- A – Anorexia (1)  
- N – Nausea/vomiting (1)  
- T – Tenderness in RLQ (2)  
- R – Rebound pain (1)  
- E – Elevated temperature >37.3°C (1)  
- L – Leucocytosis >10 × 10⁹/L (2)  
- S – Shift to left (neutrophilia >75%) (1)  
*Score interpretation: ≤4 – low probability; 5–6 – equivocal, need imaging; 7–10 – high probability, consider surgery.*

**Pattern-recognition keys for each test:**

| Test | What It Shows | Why Order It | Interpretation |
|------|--------------|-------------|----------------|
| **WBC + differential** | Neutrophilic leucocytosis (often >11 × 10⁹/L, >75% neutrophils) | No single test rules in/out; part of Alvarado | Abnormal WBC supports inflammation; normal WBC does NOT exclude appendicitis (especially children, elderly). |
| **CRP** | Elevated >10 mg/L (usually >50 mg/L) | Highly sensitive (95%) but not specific | Rising CRP from normal to high over 12–24h is strongly suggestive; very high >150 mg/L suggests perforation or abscess. |
| **Ultrasound** | Target sign (oedematous wall), non-compressible appendix >6 mm, free fluid, appendicolith | First-line in children, pregnant women; no radiation | Operator-dependent; non-visualisation of appendix does NOT rule out (15–30% failure rate). Diagnostic if seen and non-compressible. |
| **CT abdomen (with IV contrast)** | Thickened appendiceal wall (>6 mm), fat stranding, appendix enhancing, appendicolith | Gold standard; sensitivity >95%, specificity >95% | The single finding that confirms appendicitis is a dilated appendix (>6 mm) with periappendiceal fat stranding. Absence of both rules it out with high certainty. Use in adults and equivocal cases. |
| **Urine dipstick** | Normal (occasional trace protein, ketones) | Rule out UTI/renal colic; pregnancy test essential | Blood or leucocytes suggest UTI or stone; hCG+ changes management (laparoscopy vs open? MRI vs US). |

**Pattern:** In a classic case (Alvarado ≥7), proceed to theatre; in equivocal (Alvarado 4–6), get CT. In a well-appearing child/young adult with typical pain shift, some surgeons go directly to laparoscopy without imaging (but CT remains gold standard for uncertainty).

### 6. Management — Approach & Reasoning
**Immediate (first hour):**  
- **NBM** (nil by mouth) – in case of surgery, to reduce aspiration risk under anaesthesia.  
- **IV fluids** – Hartmann’s or normal saline at maintenance (e.g., 125 mL/h) – to correct dehydration from vomiting and fever.  
- **Analgesia** – **morphine 2.5–5 mg IV if needed** – safe, does not mask signs (contrary to old dogma; multiple RCTs show no effect on diagnostic accuracy).  
- **Antibiotics (if suspected complicated appendicitis or pending surgery >12h)** – **co-amoxiclav 1.2 g IV TDS** (or, if penicillin allergy, metronidazole 500 mg IV TDS + gentamicin 5–7 mg/kg IV daily). Antibiotics reduce risk of wound infection and intra-abdominal abscess.  
- **Urgent surgical referral** – discuss a plan (immediate theatre? CT first?) with registrar.  
- **Do NOT** give antiemetics before surgical review – they can mask vomiting of obstruction.  
- **Do NOT** start broad-spectrum antibiotics without confirming diagnosis unless the patient is septic – may delay definitive treatment and mask progression.

**Ward (24–72h) – if surgery chosen:**  
- **Laparoscopic appendectomy** (preferred, less pain, faster recovery). If perforated – consider open if laparoscopic skill inadequate.  
- **Postoperative:** Continue IV fluids until tolerating oral; antibiotics for 24h (or longer if perforated – 3–5 days). Analgesia: regular paracetamol 1 g QDS IV/PO + ibuprofen 400 mg TDS PO if not contraindicated.  
- **If conservative (non-surgical) approach chosen** (uncomplicated appendicitis, no appendicolith): **piperacillin-tazobactam 4.5 g IV TDS for 48h** then transition to oral co-amoxiclav 625 mg TDS for 5–7 days.

- **Monitor for failure:** worsening pain, fever, tachycardia – if no improvement in 24h, switch to surgical plan.

- For the 7–8% who recur after conservative treatment: elective laparoscopic appendectomy.  
- For those with appendiceal tumour found incidentally (carcinoid <2 cm, no metastasis): appendectomy alone is curative. Larger or mesenteric invasion needs right hemicolectomy.

**Common pitfalls:**  
- **Delaying surgery beyond 36 hours** – perforation risk increases from 20% to 70% after 48h.  
- **Giving antibiotics before CT when patient is stable** – may mask active inflammation, making diagnosis impossible.  
- **Ignoring the elderly** – early CT is the standard, not clinical scoring.  
- **Forgetting pregnancy test** in any woman of reproductive age – ectopic pregnancy can mimic appendicitis exactly, and you cannot operate without knowing.

### 7. Differential Diagnosis — Comparison Table
Comparison of appendicitis to its three most important mimics.

| Feature | Appendicitis | Gastroenteritis | Mesenteric Adenitis | Ruptured Ectopic Pregnancy |
|---------|--------------|----------------|---------------------|----------------------------|
| **Pain** | Shifting from central to RLQ; gradual onset | Crampy, diffuse, often periumbilical with diarrhoea | Lower abdominal, may be more diffuse, worse on left? | Sudden one-sided pelvic pain with shoulder tip pain |
| **Exam** | Rebound at McBurney’s; Rovsing’s; may have psoas/obturator | Diffuse tenderness without guarding; hyperactive bowel sounds | Tenderness often in RLQ but without classic peritoneal signs | Cervical motion tenderness; adnexal mass on pelvic exam; Cullen’s sign late |
| **Temperature** | Low-grade (37.5–38.5°C) | Usually low; can be high if infectious | Low-grade, but can be higher in children | Normal to low (if hypovolaemic shock) |
| **Labs** | Moderate WBC/CRP; normal LFTs | WBC variable; low CRP early; stool culture + | WBC may be mildly elevated; CRP modest | Positive β-hCG; falling haematocrit; low WBC |
| **Imaging** | US: non-compressible appendix >6 mm; CT: fat stranding | Normal appendix on CT; colonic wall thickening | CT: multiple enlarged mesenteric nodes (>5 mm), normal appendix | Ultrasound: free fluid in pouch of Douglas, adnexal mass with/without cardiac activity; β-hCG >1500 IU/L with no intrauterine pregnancy |
| **Key distinguishing feature** | Pain migration + anorexia + RLQ tenderness | Diarrhoea (watery or bloody) before pain; no anorexia | Preceding viral illness in child; normal appendix on CT | Hypotension, positive pregnancy test, adnexal mass |

### 8. Complications — What If You Miss It?

- **Perforation** – presents with sudden worsening of pain, then generalised peritonitis (diffuse guarding, rigidity, silent abdomen). Fever spikes, tachycardia. Lactate rises. *What to do:* Call senior immediately; prepare for emergency laparotomy; start IV broad-spectrum antibiotics (e.g., piperacillin-tazobactam + metronidazole); resuscitation with fluids and vasopressors as needed.
- **Appendiceal abscess** – presents 3–5 days after symptom onset with swinging pyrexia, rigors, a palpable mass in the RLQ. *Key sign:* a tender, fixed mass on deep palpation – “appendiceal phlegmon” if early, or abscess if fluctuant. *What to do:* CT with IV contrast to confirm; percutaneous drainage if >3 cm; antibiotics; delayed appendectomy (6–8 weeks). Do NOT operate acutely – risk of spreading infected material.
- **Pylephlebitis** (septic thrombophlebitis of portal vein) – rare but life-threatening. Presents with high fever, rigors, jaundice, RUQ pain, and deranged LFTs. *Key sign:* CT shows thrombus in portal vein with surrounding inflammation. *What to do:* IV antibiotics (anaerobic coverage), anticoagulation, source control (appendectomy). May require ICU.
- **Late-presentation patterns:** elderly patient presents with confusion, ileus, and low-grade fever after a week of mild pain – the appendix has already perforated and a contained abscess may have ruptured, causing generalised peritonitis. Look for a “doughy” abdomen, marked distension, and hypotension. This stem tests recognition of “missed appendicitis” – do not be fooled by absence of classic pain.

### 9. High-Yield Mnemonics & Pearls
**Mnemonic for Alvarado Score (MANTRELS):**  
M – Migration (1)  
A – Anorexia (1)  
N – Nausea/vomiting (1)  
T – Tenderness RLQ (2)  
R – Rebound (1)  
E – Elevated temperature (1)  
L – Leukocytosis (2)  
S – Shift to left (1)

**Mnemonic for signs of peritonitis:** *“Grr, R, P, O”* – Guarding, Rebound, Psoas, Obturator. Plus “Silent abdomen” (no bowel sounds in generalised peritonitis).

**Clinical Pearls (pattern-recognition cues):**
1. **Pain migration** is the most reliable single history element – if it didn’t move, think again (e.g., mesenteric adenitis, renal colic, ovulation pain).
2. **Anorexia** is almost universal – a patient who is hungry does NOT have acute appendicitis until proven otherwise.
3. **Rovsing’s sign** is specific but not sensitive; if negative, it doesn’t rule out.
4. **Psoas sign** is a red flag for retrocaecal appendix – more likely to perforate early because it’s not walled off.
5. **Elderly + RLQ tenderness + fever = appendicitis until proven otherwise** regardless of pain pattern. CT first.
6. **Pregnant + RLQ pain = always think appendicitis** because it’s the most common non-obstetric surgical emergency in pregnancy. Delay is catastrophic for both mother and foetus.
7. **CT is the gold standard** – a negative CT with good quality IV contrast virtually rules out appendicitis. Do not operate on negative ultrasound alone.

**Common exam / ward mistakes:**
- Sending patient home with “gastroenteritis” because of mild diarrhoea – remember: appendicitis can have loose stools due to irritation of adjacent bowel.
- Using a urine dip to “rule out” – negative dip does not rule in appendicitis; positive dip can be from inflamed appendix adjacent to ureter (sterile pyuria).
- In elderly, treating for UTI based on positive dipstick without imaging – the pyuria may be sterile from adjacent inflammation; they need a CT.

### 10. Escalation Criteria
**When to call your senior / ICU / crash team:**

- **NEWS2 ≥5** (or NEWS ≥7) – any sign of clinical deterioration (tachycardia >120, hypotension <90 systolic, respiratory rate >25, oxygen saturations <92% on air, confusion).  
- **Lactate >2 mmol/L** (rising or >4 mmol/L suggests shock or mesenteric ischaemia, especially if perforated).  
- **GCS <15** or any drop from baseline (think sepsis, metabolic derangement, or intracranial bleed – especially if elderly).  
- **Signs of peritonitis** – generalised guarding, rigidity, silent abdomen – call senior to review for emergency laparotomy.  
- **Pregnant patient with suspected appendicitis and any hypotension or fetal distress** – call obstetric anaesthetist and senior surgeon.  
- **Worsening pain or fever despite antibiotics** (conservative management failure) – escalate to consultant surgeon for re-assessment and possible appendectomy.

**Specific thresholds for ICU:**  
- Septic shock requiring vasopressors (e.g., noradrenaline).  
- Acute kidney injury (oliguria, creatinine doubling).  
- Post-operative respiratory failure or need for prolonged ventilation.  

In your personal practice: if you are worried, escalate early. The appendix perforates on your watch if you hesitate.
